key_insight: In TaCo$_2$Te$_2$, lattice reconstruction reshapes the low-energy electronic symmetry and, through enhanced orbital hybridization, suppresses the magnetic instability to stabilize a nonmagnetic ground state.
engineering_relevance: The work shows that structural reconstruction can be used as a design lever to tune electronic anisotropy and magnetism in low-dimensional quantum materials.
method: STM/STS and nc-AFM to map surface structure and bias-dependent electronic contrast
ARPES to measure reconstructed band structure and anisotropic Fermi surface
DFT with orbital projection to link reconstruction, hybridization, and magnetism
evidence_signal: The abstract reports consistent experimental and theoretical evidence: nc-AFM resolves a distorted hexagonal Te lattice, STM/STS reveals square-like electronic symmetry, ARPES shows reconstructed anisotropic states, and DFT reproduces the nonmagnetic stabilized ground state.
watch_item: Bias-dependent STM contrast reflects energy-integrated LDOS and orbital-specific contrast reversal, so it should not be interpreted as a direct one-to-one map of atomic positions or simple occupied/unoccupied-state inversion.
paper_title: Lattice Reconstruction and Orbital Hybridization Suppress Magnetism in TaCo$_2$Te$_2$
